title: 2025/06/26 -TI
displayTitle: 2025-06-25 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - 2025/26 Estimates for Vote Transport (Hearing with Kiwirail)
topic: 2025/26 Estimates for Vote Transport (Hearing with Kiwirail)
description: Thursday 26 June 2025 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee 2025/26 Estimates for Vote Transport (Hearing with Kiwirail) Hearing of evidence Kiwirail Peter Reidy, Chief Executive David Gordon, Chief Capital Planning and Asset Development Officer Jason Dale, Chief Financial Officer Duncan Roy, Interislander Executive General Manager Adele Wilson, Chief Customer & Growth Officer
descriptionSummary: 2025/26 Estimates for Vote Transport (Hearing with Kiwirail). Kiwirail. Peter Reidy, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Good morning, everyone. Morning, morning, morning, mayor. Um, we've, we've got you here for an hour. Thank you very much for coming in. Uh, we've, uh, we've already seen at least Dave, uh, last week, um, and, uh, board minister, and, um, so we'll, we'll, we'll give it over to you for a few minutes to, uh, to make some, any introductory comments and then look forward to the free wide ranging conversation.

Thank. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thanks for inviting us. And just introduce, uh, to me, right, CFO, Jason Dale, uh, Dale Wilson. Dale's our chief customer and, uh, growth officer. David Gordon looks after our metros and capital programs in Duncan to our left, uh, looks after her own business. So, um, apologies from our board chair because neither the acting chair or the current acting deputy chair could be here today.

Uh, Sue Tyndall has been appointed as Key Rail's chair starting one July, and we look forward to Introduc to her next time. But we are here. Um, I know my marks need to be, uh, brief, so I'll get into it pretty quick. Um, key rail is driving performance and going after growth, and we've gotta focus in three key areas, safety, performance, value, and briefly I'll go through those.

We're making a significant investment in our safety culture. We're invested in training, technology and leadership, and have an intense focus on reducing critical risks and catastrophic risks. We've had one serious harm injury so far this financial year, compared with sixth the previous year. Our near misses that could result in a serious injury or fatality have reduced 34%, uh, compared to the previous year.

Uh, however, we still have a lot to do to reduce the incident of critical risks, which are still occurring. Uh, and that is a core focus of management. All injuries have reduced by 12% in the last 12 months, and over 20% over the last two years. And about 50 to 55% of our injuries are slips, trips, and falls. So a core focus on critical risk. Let's move to performance.

We're aiming to become customers first choice for moving freight and people. We have a strong customer led strategy for growth, but this requires us to improve our reliability, customer service, labor, and asset productivity to ensure our pricing is competitive. That's how the key rail above rail business, or what we call our services business, will be sustainable.

And the goal is to have it standing on its own two feet. Uh, after a long era of managed decline, there's been 10 billion of investments since 2019 to help us build the platform for growth. It's taking time to realize the benefits of that investment. For example, even though funding for our new Reliable Loco fleet was announced in budget 2021, the first two Locos are not yet in service that we we're doing commissioning, and they'll be in service in the next couple of months.

The next couple of years, we will see a number of our assets come into service. This streamlined fleet will help us lift reliability, but we're already starting to make strides. Uh, we call our fleet reliability dfo, which is delivery in full on time for our customers. It's the number one criteria when you're in the transport game, that's your increased, your 86%, and for our larger bulk and container traffic to and from ports, it's now over 95% For customers such as fontera.

Uh, the on-time performance from our premium freight service is 90% in gradually lifting. Our reliability for the inter island has been a standout. At the end of quarter three was 98%. In May, it was a hundred. And over the last summer peak, it was over a hundred percent. I think that compares considerably against percent.

Hundred percent. It was a hundred percent. I think that compares very well against global airline businesses. We've seen a, we've seen a strong improvement from that from our customer service ratings. Freight revenue, uh, for the last year, uh, against tough economic conditions is in line with last year. Despite the significant headwinds and the closure of Windstone pulp, which was our fourth largest freight customer, our revenue from our scenic train services is 22% up on last year.

And we are on track to deliver our SCI full operating surplus target for 110 million. Uh, impact that will be impacted by the cost of redundancies for our theory, which we are working through. Now, let's turn quickly to value for money. You'll know we've embarked on a transformation of our business and work. We're working hard to control costs, lift asset utilization, lift labor productivity.

Our external costs, excluding our infrastructure projects are down 3%. Overall head count is down 7% over the past year. There's very strange to our transformation plan. And one is, and, and we, what we're doing is we're taking hundreds of ideas from a people to create savings and grow revenue. Quick example, if you have two locomotives running together for a heavy train, um, there's only a locomotive engineer or a train driver in the front one.

So when the loco's riddling the train driver, the alley has to get down from the first loco, climb into the following loco and switch it off and switch it on again when it's time to move. Now, an idea from our frontline people was that we are now doing that remotely, and by using it, we estimate we'll save 1.2 million liters of fuel a year. That's just one of the initiatives from our people.

But in the last nine months of 25, our transformation initiatives have delivered 37 million of savings and margin improvement. Lastly, let me say a little bit about metro infrastructure business. We do understand the frustration when we halt services as we recently did over and over the, uh, Easter and uh, Christmas period.

But we do that so we can work 24 hours a day to improve the network. Uh, we're not just, um, update the network in some areas. It is a complete rebuild of our foundations. We choose times of lower patrons to limit disruptions. However, we're getting results in Auckland. Our infrastructure liability for metro services is at 98.9%.

Punctuality, arriving within five minutes of the expected times is 95%. For Kiwi Rail on Wellington, our reliability is 99.6%. That means of 104,000 services that was scheduled between one July last year and Monday this week, 0.4% were canceled because of key rail.

The key rail punctuality is 95.6% in Wellington, but it's 82% when all factors not just key rail accounted. 'cause of course, we do not operate the trains in other city. In summary, um, this is a short overview of the focus on performance. We do face challenges in the domestic and global economies as our nation does, and in the complex environments, which we operate, but we are confident we're taking the right steps to build an efficient network, lift customer service, and be competitive.

So we grow and as we know, the more freight volume, we carry the greater returns on the public's investment, including benefits like reduced road con to congestion, reduced carbon, reduced maintenance, cotton and maintenance costs on that basis. Happy to take your questions, Peter. Thank you very much. Um, who's gonna kick us off? You should look over there. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you.

And we'll, we'll make a pretty free ranging in terms of where we wanna go, but if there's a particular theme, right? We'll, uh, we'll follow That with Through. No worries. Thanks Mr. Chair. Um, thanks Peter and, and the team. Um, hopefully this first question won't come as a surprise because I did raise it last week and it was parked to this week. It is about the maintenance costs for the existing fleet. Um, and we were told that that information will be provided to us today.

So could you just run me through, um, two things. One is what the maintenance schedule of the existing fleet is, um, including dry dock, uh, and what is the associated costs, um, for That. Okay. So I, I'll get Duncan to cover the, the, the program of dry dock and what we're paying for, and I'll ask Jason to cover the mainten as possible.

Thanks, Duncan. Um, so with the existing fleet, we have a strong regulatory requirement. Uh, and that includes a dry dock every two, two years. So as we move to a two ship fleet, we will see one of those ferries go to dry dock annually, and we'll be able to rotate that over a two year period.

Uh, as well as that we have what we, the, the other ferry that's not going to a dry dock. We have, we'll have a wet dock, which is an extended period of time where it's laid up in, in Wellington, where we can do other maintenance on it as well. We also have, uh, a regular once a week, 24 hour period, or there are thereabouts where other additional maintenance is done.

And that builds up the total asset management plan for, for the ferries dry dock, where it comes out of the water, where there's dig, maintenance done, wet dock, where we do, um, sort of mid-level maintenance and then day-to-day maintenance as we are either on the run or when it's tied up. Can I just ask before we move to the, the money side of things, um, what sort of timeframe are we talking about?

So when a vessel is, uh, out for dry dock, how long does it take to get to where that, I mean, we assume this is through Singapore because of the, um, defense force. Mm-hmm. Non reliability, which we understand in across Tasman. So there are no dry docks in New Zealand. Yeah, the closest one is Sydney, and that's, as you said, is, uh, controlled by Australian Defense Force, who quite Riley prioritized their own vessels.

So from a commercial perspective, we go to Singapore where we can guarantee the space and the time, uh, the transit to Singapore, the dry dock and the return, it depends, but it's a minimum a month and can sometimes go to six or seven weeks. And that can depends on what type of dry dock, because the regulatory requirements are that every second dry dock is a, is a deeper one where we, we do other types of maintenance that take longer, um, from the cost of the maintenance.

Obviously that's commercially sensitive 'cause we use the same contractors as their competition. But what I can say in 2023, there was a significant increase into 2020 fours level money spent on, on maintenance of the drive the fleet. And we have continued that through 2025 and we'll continue that there thereabouts till 29 as we maintain the safety and reliability of that.

Um, how, so you've talked about the transit, but when the vessel arrives in Singapore, how long is it actually there for? So it depends on how deep the, when you do a dry dock, you create a, a specific, what we call a specification of work with the shipyard. And so the is going away, uh, next week and that will have a, uh, be a 12 to 15 day period.

It's a relative light dry dock in the, in the docking itself, but the kataki one last year was a deeper one, and that was, uh, I think it was 21 days in the dock. So it just depends on the, the actual specification of how long the shipyard needs to do the work that you are requesting for. Okay. Yeah, and in, to answer your question, uh, it depends on the, the type of workers in the depth of the work that Duncan said.

But on an annual basis, we'd be expecting to be spending 20 to $30 million on maintenance, uh, excluding a major, you know, event where we needed to do a repair, uh, on it. So just standard maintenance work and standard dry dock activity, somewhere between 20 to $30 million in year Per per vessel?

No, for for the fleet. For the fleet or for, That's for the three at the moment. Well, this will be for the two going forward. Uh, just to clarify. Okay, so when you say there was a significant increase in 2023, no wa was that in response to, uh, the decision that had been taken by the government to cancel?

Uh, no. That was, as a result, we were unhappy with our reliability and the performance of the fleet, and we completely revamped how we do asset uh, management. That included taking another 130 crossings off and spending that time on maintenance, uh, restructure, bringing in new expertise and doing some deep obsolescence planning and replacement.

Okay. So with the decision to cancel, um, the existing theories that were on order, what, what was the response? Um, how much provision was made available for an increase in maintenance knowing that there would need to be additional levels of maintenance given that the existing fleet would be retained for longer?

So the, the change to the maintenance program was done, uh, in view that we needed to improve the reliability until 2026, which was the old program. We've continued that same level of focus through 24, 25. Okay. But you are not a, so you're not in a position to give an actual budgetary commitment around what that figure looks like for maintenance?

The, the, the cons concern of that, and the reason why it's confidential is we operate in a private market. Uh, we have suppliers and contractors out there and we have a competitor out there. So, um, I think Jason's given you a range of, of where our maintenance, um, trend has been. Uh, we've certainly for the next two years got a very clear plan of dry docking.

Um, and we have, uh, you know, the, the indicative range of what we're looking at the two ships, we are working that through, but as I said, it does come down to specification. Okay. I think I, I just clarify, within that range would be the expected program till 2020 nines spend profile, uh, absent any significant event that needed repair, that wouldn't be a standard dry dock event or maintenance event.

Okay. Right. Any sub off that one? So I just have one quick one, which is we did explore briefly, I think Dave on, uh, last week, just the benefit of having a dry dock in New Zealand. Um, so what I'm picking from that is that if you were being a month, uh, a month, um, out minimum to go to Singapore, 12 days of that being, so it's 18 days getting there and back.

Uh, so you probably knock That down, Mr. Chair, if you had a, if you had a contract today for dry dock in New Zealand would sign it this afternoon. Yeah, yeah. Well, I'm picking what from that, are you gonna be saving yourself two and a two weeks every time? It's Considerable industry and, and it, it's a, it's a, it's considerable, but you also get certainty. Yep. Um, and, uh, reduced fuel, um, reduce time out of out of Service and that would, that would come off that, um, maintenance schedule as well. Absolutely, Absolutely significant.

Any, this is off the same one Stop off. Sorry. Um, I'm just trying to do the math in my head and realized I lack clarity. Can I just go back a step? So when you said that you engaged or you initiated a program of increased maintenance to get you through to 2026, which was the, uh, expected time of the, um, canceled ferries.

So you must know what the differential is between what that increased maintenance step is that we now have to carry forward longer. Yeah. So what's that step that if you can't tell us Okay. Given commercial sensitivities, so It would is to improve immediately the performance as opposed of 23 on onwards.

Mm-hmm. Um, it would, it comes down to a mixture of a reduction in, in the revenue from taking the a hundred, 130 crossings off. And I think it was an increase of 10% of the annual budget percent, 10%. So that 10%, um, thank you. You're still on Varies. Okay.

I wanna move to contingency glands, but you come back to me. Okay. Yeah. Well, let's, Let's, let's, uh, I'll go back to you here. Yeah. Okay. Um, Julianne for primary. All good. Uh, okay. So we will be shortly down to two vessels. Um, if one is out in, in dry dock, um, annually through to 2029, what contingency plans have been put in place to address the resilience of, um, sailing opportunities for the cook strike Cover? That, so dry docking occurs, uh, we have two seasons in, in the very business we have the peak season,

which is October to May in the, in the low season. So all, all deep maintenance and dry docking and wet docks are planned in that, in that low requirement in the winter, uh, we, as we go, but by pre-planning, we are able to identify with our customers and give them warning so that those customers can work around knowing that they're our regular customers and they understand the regulatory requirements of that.

Um, and then as we go, as we go through that dry docking, we have the ability post-event, uh, 'cause we're in, we have a competitor who does the same thing to be able to ensure that, uh, that we can ensure that the customers can and get the maximum capacity for that, for that time that we're away during their maintenance period.

Adele, you wanna cover a little bit about how we manage that with, with customers? Yeah, There's, I mean, there's two situations. One is the, the dry dock, which now is planned. So, um, uh, we are confident that we can carry the freight task in the off peak season across three ships, which is ourselves across the, the total cook straight fleet.

And with the industry now being able to see those dry docks, they can plan to that as well. So it's, it's possible to do that. And, um, generally, um, you know, we are seeing comfort with that. Yesterday I was with the wholesale tourism industry. Um, they were very comfortable, uh, with the scheduling and timing of the dry docks.

Um, so the focus really is not that the focus is on, um, you know, what happens if a ship goes out during peak. Um, and you know, there is various, um, discussions underway that we're we're having on that with freight. The BCP is too coastal. Um, and there are several points that Coastal, um, can pick up freight from the north island and take it to Christchurch.

Um, so, uh, working, we've had discussions with port of toga, port of Auckland, port of toga, port of Napier, um, and of course there is new Plymouth as well. Um, and coastal regular coastal services that, um, that can, we can shift freight to, um, for tourism. Uh, you know, this is the, the BCP planning for them, um, is harder.

They need to, people are on tours, um, and, you know, when things like that happen. So we had a really good discussion about that yesterday. Um, and, you know, the need for them to look at their tours and, um, think about, you know, that flight from, um, from Wellington, your alternative there is to, um, fly people.

Um, so we are gonna have, we've agreed to have some good discussions and think about what's, what's the BCP planning we can do with the airlines, um, for their products in that space. So we're gonna continue to look at that. We are innovating new products, um, working with the rental car industry, um, as we get through these four years.

Um, and it's gonna be a process as we spoke about yesterday very positively about of continuous improvement and innovation and doing that together and us making sure we're providing good data, um, and great communication to enable them to set their businesses up for success through these next four years. Just one final question, Mr. Chair.

Um, in terms of the nature of the arrangements with Blue Bridge, we heard last week that an alternative, you know, option is, is Blue Bridge, is there a contractual arrangement in place with Blue Bridge? So Blue Bridge is a competitor and that comes with a lot of clear guidelines and what, how that works post event.

Uh, uh, we have an arrangement, uh, when either, um, in the unplanned disruption of Blue Bridge that we're able to take their customers or, or reciprocate to ensure that New Zealanders are able to get across the straight when they need to. And when that, um, arrangement has been activated, HH who, who, who needs to activate it most Bluebridge or the Inter Islander?

Well, I think over a 60 year, uh, 60 year period. I think you could have some interesting discussions on that. Look over January, uh, in inter island it very well, you know, our competitor had mechanical failures over January. We were, you know, 99% capacity, so into Ireland to really lean in there. Uh, but I think, I think you're fair to say in the last three years, I think we've all had a, we've both had a share of issues and we've both got a good relationship where we'll step in and, uh, help the market.

Thank you. And just to be clear, uh, just probably the obvious one, but, uh, there's never any dry dock. Dry dock, so, yeah. Yeah. Okay. So that makes sense. Okay, so Juliani, oh, Thank you so much.

Actually, my questions were somewhat related to, um, the being down ferries and particularly the impact of losing a rail enabled ferry, the only rail enabled ferry until we get the replacement ships. Um, I did ask some questions last week about this and heard that there wasn't too much expected impact on real freight customers.

Um, but I was wondering if you could speak well, one, like how confident are you that, um, this won't impact rail freight between the north and south island, having to do the road bridging and like what can, what gives you that confidence? And then too about kiwi rail's own like operations and needing to move, um, rail maintenance vehicles, whatever, between the north and south island? Yep. Okay.

Adele, you wanna go? So the first point is that there, there is, there's tradeoffs with the situation over the next four years, so there's absolutely no doubt about that. Those tradeoffs are in, um, the capacity, total rail capacity across the ferries. So it's a reduction of, um, from 32 wagons down to 24.

So that's, that's the first, you know, really tangible impact. Um, and that 24 is a time constraint. So that's about how long it takes to operate the turn, um, for rail, uh, with a road bridge solution. Um, and there's also a cost to that, uh, you know, a significant additional cost. Um, it requires different equipment, more people, um, and we have, um, scaled up, uh, in order to be able to provide a service and keep our time gate, which is critical for the market, the freight market from Auckland to Christchurch.

Um, so those are the trade-offs. It's time and money. Um, and, you know, we're working through that at the moment. How do we know it's gonna work? Well, our people have a lot of experience. We, we road bridge today. Um, so we have a good sense of it. The key to the next four years is about having a really strong continuous improvement mindset and about, um, you know, we are gonna go into this.

We, we are well placed, we understand our operation well in the modeling. Um, we are confident in it and our people are confident. Um, and what we're gonna have to be good at is learning as we go through and adjusting and being agile to get better and better over the, over the four years to try and minimize impacts. Um, and it's necessary.

You know, we need these new, uh, ships, we've got to get through this. Um, and so far, you know, the cooperation with the industry, uh, with both freight and tourism passenger has been excellent. And, you know, we're seeing a real spirit of cooperation and a and a spirit of we will get through this together with great communication, um, and taking a a learning approach.

Can I mm-hmm. Yeah. I mean, you might wanna say more, but like 32 to 24, that's like a 25% reduction in capacity. Yeah. Um, is there space mm-hmm. Like, I mean, can that just shift to trucks or is there not even really capacity for an increase in freight shifting to trucks on this ferries?

Like will it be a shift to coastal shipping, do you think? Um, look, we, we think over the day, so it's the premium train that that runs at night, which is our 19 hour service between Auckland and Christchurch, um, where, you know, we will feel the constraint, but we've been working with our customers and we have been able to, not all freight needs to move quickly.

Mm-hmm. Um, so when we look at our services over the day, we can handle the freight task and we have some headroom, um, across all of our train services. So we're comfortable with that. We have worked with our customers and some of that, that freight will spread onto our slower service, um, Auckland to Christchurch.

Um, and that's all looking fine. There is, it does put a constraint on us, uh, in the next, over the next four years. Um, but road is the alternative. Um, and, you know, freight can shift onto road. Um, so that's that. I think coastal is, in our view the BCP solution and I think we've got plenty of time for those months, you know, that the dry docks are in place, um, for people to plan that in advance and plan the freight task in advance.

And that's the key thing in logistics, when people have got time, um, you know, those arrangements can be made quite easily actually. Um, but coastals, um, disadvantages time and, you know, it is the slowest, it is the slowest mode, uh, in the least reliable mode just because of shipping and Limited capacity. That's right. So it's, um, we think, we think, you know, we can do it with shifted freight effectively and at the moment, um, yeah, we are happy with where we sit with our planning with customers. we just talk, don't talk about moving tampers, uh, machines, EMA over to the south island. Yeah, I thing, as I said last week, we're in a program at the moment of moving what we can south now. We will have to be deck cargo And as we start to position in the next four years, you know, we will be setting up, for example, rail welding facilities in South island. Um, and, you know, we'll have, uh, particular, uh, tampers and equipment based in the south island for the longer term.

Doug was there. Uh, yeah. So it is more rail, uh, road bridging is more complex and does take additional time.
